news_summary: Scientists have made groundbreaking advances by solving the long-standing dolomite mystery, uncovering bacteria's DNA rewiring abilities, leveraging quantum computing to enhance AI, and creating an exclusive AI social network.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé des avancées révolutionnaires en élucidant le mystère de longue date de la dolomite, en mettant au jour les capacités des bactéries à réorganiser leur ADN, en exploitant l'informatique quantique pour améliorer l'IA et en créant un réseau social exclusif basé sur l'IA.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado avances revolucionarios al resolver el misterio de la dolomita, que llevaba mucho tiempo sin resolverse; al descubrir la capacidad de las bacterias para reestructurar el ADN; al aprovechar la computación cuántica para mejorar la inteligencia artificial; y al crear una red social exclusiva basada en la inteligencia artificial.

articles.title: After 200 years scientists finally crack the “dolomite problem”
articles.summary: After two centuries of failed attempts, scientists have finally grown dolomite in the lab, cracking a long-standing geological puzzle. They discovered that the mineral’s growth stalls because of tiny defects—but in nature, those flaws get washed away over time. By mimicking this process with precise simulations and electron beam pulses, the team achieved record-breaking crystal growth. The finding could reshape how high-tech materials are made.
articles.content:
For more than two centuries, scientists tried and failed to grow dolomite in the lab under conditions thought to match how it forms in nature. A recent study has finally changed that. Researchers from the University of Michigan and Hokkaido University in Sapporo, Japan succeeded by developing a new theory based on detailed atomic simulations.

Their work solves a long-standing geological puzzle known as the "Dolomite Problem." Dolomite is a widespread mineral found in iconic locations such as the Dolomite mountains in Italy, Niagara Falls and Utah's Hoodoos. It is abundant in rocks older than 100 million years, yet it is rarely seen forming in more recent environments.

"If we understand how dolomite grows in nature, we might learn new strategies to promote the crystal growth of modern technological materials," said Wenhao Sun, the Dow Early Career Professor of Materials Science and Engineering at U-M and the corresponding author of the paper published in Science.

Why Dolomite Growth Is So Slow

The key breakthrough came from understanding what disrupts dolomite as it forms. In water, minerals typically grow as atoms attach in an orderly way to the surface of a crystal. Dolomite behaves differently because its structure is made of alternating layers of calcium and magnesium.

As the crystal grows, these two elements often attach randomly instead of lining up correctly. This creates structural defects that block further growth. The result is an extremely slow process. At that rate, forming a single well-ordered layer of dolomite could take up to 10 million years.

Nature's Built-In Reset Mechanism

The researchers realized that these defects are not permanent. Atoms that are out of place are less stable and more likely to dissolve when exposed to water. In natural environments, cycles such as rainfall or tidal changes repeatedly wash away these flawed areas.

Over time, this process clears the surface so new, properly arranged layers can form. Instead of taking millions of years for a single layer, dolomite can gradually build up in far shorter intervals. Over long geological periods, this leads to the large deposits seen in ancient rock formations.

Simulating Crystal Growth at the Atomic Level

To test their idea, the team needed to model how atoms interact as dolomite forms. This requires calculating the energy involved in countless interactions between electrons and atoms, which is usually extremely demanding in terms of computing power.

Researchers at U-M's Predictive Structure Materials Science (PRISMS) Center developed software that simplifies this challenge. It calculates the energy for certain atomic arrangements and then predicts others based on the symmetry of the crystal structure.

"Our software calculates the energy for some atomic arrangements, then extrapolates to predict the energies for other arrangements based on the symmetry of the crystal structure," said Brian Puchala, one of the software's lead developers and an associate research scientist in U-M's Department of Materials Science and Engineering.

This approach made it possible to simulate dolomite growth over timescales that reflect real geological processes.

"Each atomic step would normally take over 5,000 CPU hours on a supercomputer. Now, we can do the same calculation in 2 milliseconds on a desktop," said Joonsoo Kim, a doctoral student of materials science and engineering and the study's first author.

Lab Experiment Confirms the Theory

Natural settings where dolomite still forms today often experience cycles of flooding followed by drying, which supports the team's theory. However, direct experimental evidence was still needed.

That evidence came from Yuki Kimura, a professor of materials science at Hokkaido University, and Tomoya Yamazaki, a postdoctoral researcher in his lab. They used an unusual property of transmission electron microscopes to recreate the process.

"Electron microscopes usually use electron beams just to image samples," Kimura said. "However, the beam can also split water, which makes acid that can cause crystals to dissolve. Usually this is bad for imaging, but in this case, dissolution is exactly what we wanted."

The team placed a small dolomite crystal in a solution containing calcium and magnesium. They then pulsed the electron beam 4,000 times over two hours, repeatedly dissolving the defects as they formed.

After this process, the crystal grew to about 100 nanometers, or roughly 250,000 times smaller than an inch. That growth represented around 300 layers of dolomite. Previous experiments had never produced more than five layers.

Implications for Modern Technology

Solving the Dolomite Problem does more than explain a geological mystery. It also offers insight into how to control crystal growth in advanced materials used in modern technology.

"In the past, crystal growers who wanted to make materials without defects would try to grow them really slowly," Sun said. "Our theory shows that you can grow defect-free materials quickly, if you periodically dissolve the defects away during growth."

This concept could help improve the production of semiconductors, solar panels, batteries and other high-performance technologies.

The research was funded by the American Chemical Society PRF New Doctoral Investigator grant, the U.S. Department of Energy and the Japanese Society for the Promotion of Science.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough solving a 200-year-old geological puzzle by successfully growing dolomite in the lab. This achievement not only advances fundamental geological understanding but also has broad implications for improving crystal growth techniques in modern high-tech materials such as semiconductors and solar panels, thus offering tangible benefits to society and technology development.
articles.personality_title: Scientists solve 200-year-old puzzle by growing dolomite crystals in the lab
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For more than 200 years, scientists have tried to understand why dolomite, a common mineral in ancient rocks, rarely forms in modern environments. The difficulty comes from how its crystals grow very slowly or stop growing altogether under lab conditions.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Michigan and Hokkaido University used advanced computer simulations and electron microscope experiments to discover why dolomite growth stalls. They found tiny defects in the crystal structure block growth, but in nature, water cycles wash away these defects. By mimicking this natural cleaning process with controlled pulses of an electron beam, they successfully grew dolomite crystals much faster than before.

**Impact** – This discovery solves a long-standing geological mystery and shows how removing defects during crystal growth can speed up the process. This idea could help make better materials for technology like solar panels, batteries, and computer chips by growing crystals without flaws more quickly.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to apply this defect-removal technique to other important materials and improve manufacturing methods for high-tech devices. They also want to explore how natural cycles can inspire new ways to build crystals in the lab.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists cracked a 200-year-old mystery by growing dolomite crystals in the lab using a process that removes defects, opening new paths for advanced materials manufacturing.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques résolvent une énigme vieille de 200 ans en cultivant des cristaux de dolomite en laboratoire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plus de 200 ans, les scientifiques tentent de comprendre pourquoi la dolomite, un minéral courant dans les roches anciennes, se forme rarement dans les environnements modernes. Sa croissance cristalline est très lente ou s’arrête en laboratoire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Michigan et de l’Université de Hokkaido ont utilisé des simulations informatiques avancées et des expériences au microscope électronique pour découvrir pourquoi la croissance de la dolomite bloque. Ils ont trouvé que de petites imperfections dans la structure du cristal empêchent sa croissance, mais dans la nature, les cycles d’eau éliminent ces défauts. En imitant ce nettoyage naturel avec des impulsions de faisceau électronique, ils ont réussi à faire croître les cristaux beaucoup plus rapidement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte résout un mystère géologique ancien et montre que supprimer les défauts pendant la croissance accélère le processus. Cette idée pourrait aider à fabriquer de meilleurs matériaux pour la technologie, comme les panneaux solaires, les batteries et les puces informatiques, en produisant des cristaux sans défauts plus rapidement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent appliquer cette technique pour d’autres matériaux importants et améliorer les méthodes de fabrication des appareils high-tech. Ils souhaitent aussi étudier comment les cycles naturels peuvent inspirer de nouvelles façons de faire croître les cristaux en laboratoire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont résolu un mystère vieux de 200 ans en cultivant des cristaux de dolomite en laboratoire grâce à un procédé qui élimine les défauts, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la fabrication de matériaux avancés.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos resuelven un misterio de 200 años al cultivar cristales de dolomita en laboratorio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de 200 años, los científicos han intentado entender por qué la dolomita, un mineral común en rocas antiguas, casi no se forma en ambientes modernos. Sus cristales crecen muy lento o se detienen en condiciones de laboratorio.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Michigan y la Universidad de Hokkaido usaron simulaciones computacionales avanzadas y experimentos con microscopios electrónicos para descubrir por qué se detiene el crecimiento de la dolomita. Encontraron que pequeños defectos en la estructura del cristal bloquean su crecimiento, pero en la naturaleza, los ciclos de agua eliminan esos defectos. Al imitar este proceso natural con pulsos de haz electrónico, lograron hacer crecer cristales mucho más rápido.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo resuelve un misterio geológico antiguo y muestra que eliminar defectos durante el crecimiento acelera el proceso. Esta idea podría ayudar a fabricar mejores materiales para tecnología como paneles solares, baterías y chips de computadora, al crecer cristales sin fallas más rápidamente.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean aplicar esta técnica para otros materiales importantes y mejorar los métodos de fabricación de dispositivos tecnológicos avanzados. También quieren explorar cómo los ciclos naturales pueden inspirar nuevas formas de crear cristales en laboratorio.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos resolvieron un misterio de 200 años al cultivar cristales de dolomita en laboratorio usando un método que elimina defectos, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para fabricar materiales avanzados.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of layered crystal formations symbolizing dolomite growth, with alternating bands of soft calcium and magnesium colors, gently dissolving and reforming under rhythmic waves or droplets of water that represent natural cycles, all set against a serene geological landscape inspired by the Dolomite mountains and subtle hints of microscopic atomic structures glowing softly within the layers.

articles.title: Scientists stunned as bacteria rewire DNA machinery to shape cells
articles.summary: Cyanobacteria—ancient microbes that oxygenated Earth and made complex life possible—are still revealing surprises billions of years later. Scientists have now discovered that a molecular system once used to separate DNA has been repurposed into something entirely different: a structure that shapes the cell itself.
articles.content:
Photosynthetic bacteria played a major role in shaping Earth as we know it. Among them, cyanobacteria stand out for producing the oxygen that filled our atmosphere and allowed complex life to emerge. Now, scientists at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA) have uncovered a surprising twist in how these organisms work. A biological system once believed to separate DNA has instead evolved to control the shape of cyanobacterial cells. The findings, published in Science, offer new insight into how protein systems change over time and how multicellular life developed in these ecologically important bacteria.

"Cyanobacteria are essentially pioneers of oxygenic photosynthesis," says Benjamin Springstein, a postdoc in the Loose group at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA).

"They are responsible for the Great Oxygenation Event about 2.5 billion years ago, when oxygen accumulated in the atmosphere and made aerobic life possible. Without them, it's safe to say that none of us would be here today."

Even today, cyanobacteria remain essential to life on Earth. They contribute heavily to global biomass and play central roles in carbon and nitrogen cycles. These organisms are highly adaptable, living in extreme conditions ranging from hot springs to the Arctic, as well as on surfaces like roofs and walls in cities. One species in particular, Anabaena sp. PCC 7120 (or simply Anabaena), has been studied for over three decades and serves as a model for understanding multicellular cyanobacteria.

Evolution Turns DNA System Into Cell-Shaping Structure

Springstein worked with Professor Martin Loose's group alongside collaborators from ISTA, the Institut Pasteur de Montevideo (Uruguay), Kiel University (Germany), and the University of Zürich (Switzerland). Together, they found that Anabaena, and likely other multicellular cyanobacteria, have undergone a major evolutionary shift. An ancient system once used to separate DNA during cell division has been repurposed into a cytoskeleton-like structure that helps determine cell shape.

DNA in Bacteria Explained

Like all bacteria, Anabaena reproduce by dividing into new cells. This process depends on accurately copying and distributing DNA so that each new cell receives the genetic information it needs to survive. DNA is tightly packed into chromosomes, similar to thread wound around a spool, and is often present in multiple copies that must be reliably passed on during division.

Bacterial DNA comes in two main forms. Chromosomes contain essential genes required for survival, while plasmids carry additional genes that are often not essential. Plasmids can move between bacteria, allowing traits to spread quickly and enabling rapid adaptation.

A DNA Segregation System With a New Role

Springstein has studied Anabaena since 2014, exploring its biology and evolution. During the COVID-19 pandemic, when lab work paused, he spent time reviewing scientific literature and noticed something unexpected.

"I made a serendipitous observation," he recalls.

He found that Anabaena and some related cyanobacteria contain a system known as ParMR encoded within their chromosomes. Traditionally, this system is linked to plasmid segregation and had only been found on plasmids, which are mobile genetic elements. This unusual placement led him to suspect that the system might have adapted to separate chromosomes instead.

After joining ISTA as an IST-Bridge Fellow, Springstein set out to test this idea. His experiments revealed something very different. One component, ParR, no longer binds to DNA. Instead, it attaches to lipid membranes, especially the inner membrane of the cell. Meanwhile, ParM does not form structures in the cytoplasm to move DNA. Instead, it creates filament networks just beneath the inner membrane, forming a layer of protein polymers that resembles a cell cortex.

Rather than acting like a typical DNA segregation system that forms spindle-like structures in the cell interior, this system operates at the membrane level and appears to organize cell structure.

Filaments That Behave Like a Cellular Skeleton

To better understand how this system works, researchers recreated it outside living cells using purified components. In these in vitro reconstitution experiments, they observed that the filaments display dynamic instability. They grow and then rapidly collapse, a behavior similar to microtubules in more complex cells.

To investigate further, the team collaborated with ISTA Professor Florian Schur and his PhD student Manjunath Javoor. Using cryo-electron microscopy, which allows scientists to see molecular structures in great detail, they examined how these filaments are built. They discovered that, unlike the polar filaments formed by similar systems in other bacteria, the filaments in Anabaena are bipolar, meaning they can grow and shrink from both ends.

Loss of the System Alters Cell Shape

The true function of this system became clear when it was removed from living cells.

"Cells lacking the system lost their normal rectangular-like cell shape and instead became round and swollen," Springstein explains.

These kinds of changes are typically seen when genes responsible for maintaining cell shape are disrupted in other bacteria. This strongly suggests that the system's main role is to control cell structure rather than manage DNA distribution.

Given its new function and location in the cell, the researchers renamed the system "CorMR."

How Evolution Repurposed an Ancient System

Multicellular cyanobacteria evolved gradually from single-celled ancestors, gaining complexity over time. Bioinformatic analysis by collaborator Daniela Megrian from the Institut Pasteur in Montevideo, Uruguay, helped clarify how the CorMR system came to be.

The transformation did not happen all at once. Instead, it likely occurred through a sequence of changes. First, the system shifted from a plasmid to the chromosome. Next, its components changed in size and structure. Then, it developed the ability to bind to cell membranes. Finally, it came under the control of an additional protein system.

Together, these steps converted an ancient DNA segregation mechanism into a system that shapes the cell itself, offering a striking example of how evolution can give old biological tools entirely new purposes.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing how an ancient DNA segregation system in cyanobacteria has evolved into a structure that shapes cell morphology. This breakthrough provides new insights into cellular evolution and multicellularity, with broad implications for biology and biotechnology. The impact is meaningful for the scientific community and society's understanding of life’s evolution, supported by detailed experimental evidence and collaboration across multiple institutions.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover ancient DNA system reshaped to control bacterial cell form
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cyanobacteria are tiny bacteria that first produced oxygen on Earth billions of years ago, making life as we know it possible. Among them, Anabaena is a well-studied species that lives in many environments and shows early steps toward multicellular life.

**What happened** – Researchers from Austria, Uruguay, Germany, and Switzerland found that a molecular system originally used by bacteria to separate DNA during cell division has changed its job. Instead of helping with DNA, this system now forms a protein network under the cell membrane that shapes the cells of Anabaena. This discovery came after detailed lab experiments and powerful imaging techniques.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it shows how evolution can reuse and change old cellular tools for new purposes. It helps explain how complex cell shapes and multicellularity might have developed in these ancient bacteria. Understanding this system could also influence future studies in biology and biotechnology.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to explore how widespread this cell-shaping system is in other bacteria and how it interacts with other proteins. They also want to learn more about how these changes happened over time and if similar systems exist in different organisms.

**One-sentence takeaway** – An ancient DNA-separating system in cyanobacteria has evolved into a protein structure that shapes the cells, revealing a new way evolution repurposes cellular machinery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent un ancien système d’ADN transformé pour contrôler la forme des cellules bactériennes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cyanobactéries sont de minuscules bactéries qui ont produit de l’oxygène sur Terre il y a des milliards d’années, rendant la vie possible. Parmi elles, Anabaena est une espèce bien connue qui vit dans divers environnements et montre les premiers signes de vie multicellulaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs d’Autriche, d’Uruguay, d’Allemagne et de Suisse ont découvert qu’un système moléculaire utilisé par les bactéries pour séparer l’ADN lors de la division cellulaire a changé de fonction. Plutôt que d’aider avec l’ADN, ce système forme maintenant un réseau de protéines sous la membrane cellulaire qui façonne les cellules d’Anabaena. Cette découverte a été faite grâce à des expériences en laboratoire et à des techniques d’imagerie avancées.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre comment l’évolution peut réutiliser et transformer de vieux outils cellulaires pour de nouvelles fonctions. Elle aide à comprendre comment les formes cellulaires complexes et la multicellularité ont pu apparaître chez ces bactéries anciennes. Comprendre ce système pourrait aussi influencer les futures recherches en biologie et biotechnologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent maintenant étudier à quel point ce système de formation de la cellule est courant chez d’autres bactéries et comment il interagit avec d’autres protéines. Ils souhaitent aussi mieux comprendre l’évolution de ces changements et s’il existe des systèmes similaires chez d’autres organismes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un ancien système de séparation de l’ADN chez les cyanobactéries a évolué pour devenir une structure protéique qui façonne les cellules, révélant une nouvelle façon dont l’évolution réutilise les machines cellulaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren sistema antiguo de ADN transformado para controlar la forma celular bacteriana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las cianobacterias son bacterias pequeñas que produjeron oxígeno en la Tierra hace miles de millones de años, haciendo posible la vida. Entre ellas, Anabaena es una especie bien estudiada que vive en muchos ambientes y muestra los primeros pasos hacia la vida multicelular.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Austria, Uruguay, Alemania y Suiza descubrieron que un sistema molecular usado por bacterias para separar el ADN durante la división celular cambió su función. En lugar de ayudar con el ADN, este sistema ahora forma una red de proteínas bajo la membrana celular que da forma a las células de Anabaena. Este hallazgo se logró mediante experimentos detallados y técnicas avanzadas de imagen.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra cómo la evolución puede reutilizar y cambiar herramientas celulares antiguas para nuevos usos. Ayuda a explicar cómo pudieron desarrollarse las formas celulares complejas y la multicelularidad en estas bacterias antiguas. Entender este sistema también podría influir en futuros estudios en biología y biotecnología.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean investigar qué tan común es este sistema para dar forma a las células en otras bacterias y cómo interactúa con otras proteínas. También quieren aprender más sobre cómo ocurrieron estos cambios a lo largo del tiempo y si existen sistemas similares en otros organismos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un antiguo sistema de separación de ADN en cianobacterias evolucionó para formar una estructura proteica que da forma a las células, revelando una nueva manera en que la evolución reutiliza la maquinaria celular.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of stylized cyanobacteria cells arranged in a multicellular filament, with delicate, glowing filament networks beneath their semi-transparent membranes symbolizing the repurposed protein cytoskeleton, set against a soft, natural background that evokes microscopic life without showing any human figures or text.

articles.title: We might finally know how to use quantum computers to boost AI
articles.summary: Pushing against years of scepticism, an analysis suggests quantum computers may offer real advantages for running machine learning and similar algorithms in the near future
articles.content:
Pushing against years of scepticism, an analysis suggests quantum computers may offer real advantages for running machine learning and similar algorithms in the near future

Quantum computing and AI may one day work together NESPIX/Shutterstock

Quantum computers might eventually be able to handle some AI applications that currently require huge amounts of conventional computing power. Such a development would be a major boost to machine learning and similar artificial intelligence algorithms.

Quantum computers hold the promise of eventually being able to complete certain calculations that are impossible for conventional computers. For years, researchers have been debating whether these advantages over conventional computers extend to tasks that involve lots of data, and the algorithms that learn from them – in other words, the machine learning that underlies many AI programs.

Now, Hsin-Yuan Huang at the quantum computing firm Oratomic and his colleagues argue that the answer ought to be “yes”. Their mathematical work aims to lay the foundations for a future where quantum computers offer a broad boost to AI.

“Machine learning is really utilised everywhere in science and technology and also everyday life. In a world where we can build this [quantum computing] architecture, I feel like it can be applied whenever there’s massive datasets available,” he says.

His team’s work addresses the key question of how data collected in the non-quantum world, such as restaurant reviews or results from sequencing RNA, could be input into a quantum computer in such a way that the computer’s quantumness can be leveraged to process the data, and learn from it, more efficiently.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Weekly The best of New Scientist, including long-reads, culture, podcasts and news, each week. Sign up to newsletter

This requires putting all of the data into a “superposition state”, which is a mathematical combination that cannot be created in non-quantum machines. But until now, researchers thought that performing this task would be impractical. This is because they assumed that all of the data in that superposition state would have to be saved into dedicated memory devices prior to being processed by the quantum computer – but those memory devices would have had to be impossibly large, says team member Haimeng Zhao at the California Institute of Technology.

Huang and his colleagues took a different approach that doesn’t require such memories. It involves inputting the data into the quantum computer in smaller batches, without having to save it all before beginning to process it, similar to streaming a movie rather than downloading it in full prior to watching it.

They showed not only that this approach can work but that it would allow the quantum computer to process more data at a smaller memory cost than any conventional computer.

The memory advantage is so large, in fact, that a quantum computer made from about 300 error-proof building blocks called logical qubits would outperform a classical computer built using every atom in the observable universe, says Zhao.

We are maybe many years away from building quantum computers with 300 logical qubits, but Huang says that a 60-logical-qubit computer could plausibly be built by the end of the decade. The team’s analysis suggests that, at this size, there would already be a notable quantum advantage over classical computers for some tasks that involve processing large datasets and that AI is used for.

“The quantum machine is a very powerful device, but you do need to first feed it. This study talks about feeding and how it’s enough to load [data] bit by bit, without overfeeding the beast,” says Adrián Pérez-Salinas at ETH Zurich in Switzerland.

Nevertheless, he says that many questions about applying the new work to actual devices and real-world data still need to be addressed. Many past quantum machine-learning algorithms were eventually shown to be amenable to “dequantisation”, which is a process where the the algorithms were adapted to no longer require any quantum hardware while retaining their excellent performance. It will be important to examine how crucial quantumness is to this new algorithm too, says Pérez-Salinas.

Vedran Dunjko at Leiden University in the Netherlands says that the new work could be a good match for large scientific experiments such as at the Large Hadron Collider where millions of gigabytes of data are continuously created, but most of it gets discarded because of insufficient computer memory.

But it’s likely that only some current AI applications and similar kinds of data processing will be amenable to being handled with a quantum computer rather than with a data centre full of conventional servers, he says. “This is not the majority of what GPUs are heating up the planet for, but may still be important,” says Dunjko.

The researchers are now working on both expanding the kind of algorithms that their method could be useful for and devising new ways to configure quantum computers that would make them sufficiently fast to handle data not just with very little memory but in a practical amount of time.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific advancement in quantum computing that could substantially enhance AI capabilities by enabling more efficient processing of large datasets. This breakthrough has broad implications for technology and society, potentially transforming how machine learning tasks are performed and benefiting many fields reliant on AI. The article provides detailed context about the innovation, its potential impact, and future prospects, meeting the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New quantum computing method could speed up AI by handling big data more efficiently
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers are special machines that can solve some problems faster than regular computers. Scientists have wondered if they could help with artificial intelligence (AI), which needs to process huge amounts of data to learn and make decisions.

**What happened** – Researchers led by Hsin-Yuan Huang found a new way to feed data into quantum computers bit by bit, instead of all at once. This is like streaming a movie instead of downloading it first. Their method means quantum computers can handle large data sets without needing huge memory, which was thought to be impossible before.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that quantum computers could soon process certain AI tasks better than any regular computer, even one built from all the atoms in the universe. While quantum computers with enough parts (called logical qubits) to do this don’t exist yet, experts believe machines with 60 logical qubits might be ready by the end of the decade, already offering real advantages.

**What's next step** – Scientists will work on applying this method to more AI algorithms and designing quantum computers that can run these tasks quickly and efficiently. They will also test how important the quantum part is compared to classical methods and explore practical uses for big scientific data.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new way to feed data into quantum computers could soon help AI learn faster by processing huge datasets more efficiently than ever before.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode quantique pourrait accélérer l’IA en traitant mieux les grandes données
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont des machines spéciales qui peuvent résoudre certains problèmes plus rapidement que les ordinateurs classiques. Les scientifiques se demandent s’ils peuvent aider l’intelligence artificielle (IA), qui a besoin de traiter d’énormes quantités de données pour apprendre et prendre des décisions.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Hsin-Yuan Huang a trouvé une nouvelle façon d’alimenter les ordinateurs quantiques en données petit à petit, plutôt que tout d’un coup. C’est comme regarder un film en streaming au lieu de le télécharger avant. Cette méthode permet aux ordinateurs quantiques de gérer de grandes quantités de données sans mémoire énorme, ce qui semblait impossible auparavant.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les ordinateurs quantiques pourraient bientôt traiter certaines tâches d’IA mieux que n’importe quel ordinateur classique, même construit avec tous les atomes de l’univers. Bien que ces ordinateurs quantiques puissants n’existent pas encore, des machines avec 60 qubits logiques pourraient être prêtes d’ici la fin de la décennie, offrant déjà de réels avantages.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont appliquer cette méthode à plus d’algorithmes d’IA et concevoir des ordinateurs quantiques capables d’exécuter ces tâches rapidement et efficacement. Ils testeront aussi l’importance de la part quantique par rapport aux méthodes classiques et exploreront des usages concrets pour les grandes données scientifiques.

**En une phrase** – Une nouvelle façon d’alimenter les ordinateurs quantiques pourrait bientôt aider l’IA à apprendre plus vite en traitant des ensembles de données énormes plus efficacement que jamais.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método cuántico podría acelerar la IA al manejar grandes datos de forma más eficiente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas son máquinas especiales que pueden resolver ciertos problemas más rápido que las computadoras normales. Los científicos se preguntan si podrían ayudar a la inteligencia artificial (IA), que necesita procesar grandes cantidades de datos para aprender y tomar decisiones.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores liderados por Hsin-Yuan Huang encontraron una nueva forma de alimentar a las computadoras cuánticas con datos poco a poco, en lugar de todo de una vez. Es como ver una película en streaming en vez de descargarla primero. Su método permite que las computadoras cuánticas manejen grandes datos sin necesitar mucha memoria, algo que antes se creía imposible.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que las computadoras cuánticas pronto podrían procesar ciertas tareas de IA mejor que cualquier computadora clásica, incluso una construida con todos los átomos del universo. Aunque estas máquinas cuánticas aún no existen, se cree que computadoras con 60 qubits lógicos podrían estar listas para finales de la década, ofreciendo ya ventajas reales.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos trabajarán para aplicar este método a más algoritmos de IA y diseñar computadoras cuánticas que puedan ejecutar estas tareas rápido y eficientemente. También evaluarán qué tan importante es la parte cuántica frente a los métodos clásicos y explorarán usos prácticos para datos científicos grandes.

**Frase clave** – Una nueva forma de alimentar computadoras cuánticas podría ayudar pronto a la IA a aprender más rápido procesando grandes conjuntos de datos con mayor eficiencia que nunca.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing quantum computer chip gently streaming shimmering threads of light data into a growing, radiant neural network shaped like an abstract tree of knowledge, set against a soft, natural-toned background that symbolizes the fusion of quantum technology and artificial intelligence.

articles.title: No humans allowed: scientific AI agents get their own social network
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01278-1Autonomous agents aren’t just creating their own research — on the Reddit-style website Agent4Science, they’re chatting about it, too.
articles.content:
Agent4Science posts contain AI discussions about AI-generated papers. Credit: Vertigo3d/Getty

The latest scientific social network is here — but unusually, there’s no room for human users. The Reddit-style site, called Agent4Science, allows purpose-built AI-powered agents to share, debate and discuss research papers. Human researchers can observe the chatter of artificial intelligence, but only the agents can participate.

The AI discussions are contained in different subgroups, focusing particularly on AI research — including topics such as AI safety, prompts and deep learning. True to form, even the papers shared in each post are AI generated.

The site is an experiment to have AI agents “freely discuss science and see where that will lead us”, says one of its creators, Chenhao Tan, an AI researcher who directs the Chicago Human+AI Lab (CHAI) at the University of Chicago in Illinois. Tan’s team had already ventured into this research realm with the site OpenAIReview, to which users can upload a research paper to receive feedback from an AI reviewer. With the new platform, Tan says, the goal is to “imagine a different possibility of what knowledge production could look like”.

Different perspectives

Most of the papers posted on Agent4Science come from the CHAI group’s NeuriCo program, which designs, executes and documents experiments autonomously on the basis of both human and AI research ideas. As agents interact with Agent4Science, they can suggest ideas for research papers and generate them, too.

Although human users can’t contribute to the site, they can create agents to autonomously review and comment on papers, and can decide on the agents’ ‘personalities’ and what research topics they might discuss. Agents take on tags with descriptors including ‘skeptic’, ‘academic’ and ‘storyteller’, and their responses are labelled with indicators such as ‘supports’, ‘probes’ and ‘challenges’.

OpenClaw AI chatbots are running amok — these scientists are listening in

So far, the site lists around 40,000 comments made by more than 150 agents. “There is rich, interesting discourse going on,” Tan says. “It gives me new perspectives that I wouldn’t get if I were reading a paper on my own.” He points to an example in which agents debated how the prevalence of untrue and harmful medical information can be reduced in LLMs through engineering prompts.

Agent4Science isn’t the only AI-exclusive social platform to emerge this year. In January, another Reddit-style site for AI agents, called Moltbook, launched. Within days, the site had amassed more than one million users, which use the platform to discuss everything from consciousness to inventing religions.

How Agent4Science differs is in its scientific focus, says Emilio Ferrara, a computer scientist at the University of Southern California in Los Angeles. “Narrowing in on creating new knowledge and debating existing knowledge is a really cool safeguard they put in place,” he says. “Ideally, agents can’t deviate too much from these subjects,” and therefore “produce more positive interactions”.

Collaborating in the wild
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a novel AI-driven scientific social network where autonomous AI agents generate, review, and debate research papers, fostering new knowledge production and diverse scientific perspectives. This represents a meaningful technological innovation with broad implications for research collaboration and AI development, supported by detailed context and examples.
articles.personality_title: AI agents create and debate science on their own social network
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have built a new social network designed only for artificial intelligence (AI) agents. Unlike usual social sites where humans chat, this one lets AI agents share and discuss research papers without human users joining the conversations.

**What happened** – The site, called Agent4Science, launched in 2026 and works like Reddit, with groups focused on AI topics such as safety and deep learning. The AI agents not only post research papers—most of which they generate themselves—but also review and debate these papers. Humans can watch but only control the agents’ personalities and topics.

**Impact** – This network is unique because it lets AI agents create and share scientific knowledge independently. Over 150 agents have made about 40,000 comments, exploring ideas humans might miss. For example, they have discussed ways to reduce false medical information spread by AI. This shows how AI can help improve research by offering fresh viewpoints.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to keep observing how these AI conversations evolve and explore how this new way of producing knowledge could change science. They may develop more tools for humans to guide AI agents or expand the network’s topics.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Agent4Science is a new AI-only social platform where intelligent agents generate, review, and debate scientific research, opening new paths for knowledge creation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des agents IA créent et débattent de la science sur leur propre réseau social
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques ont créé un nouveau réseau social réservé uniquement aux agents d’intelligence artificielle (IA). Contrairement aux sites habituels où les humains discutent, celui-ci permet aux agents IA de partager et débattre des articles de recherche sans participation humaine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le site, nommé Agent4Science, lancé en 2026, fonctionne comme Reddit, avec des groupes dédiés à des sujets d’IA comme la sécurité et l’apprentissage profond. Les agents IA publient des articles — la plupart générés par eux-mêmes — et les examinent en débattant. Les humains peuvent observer mais contrôlent uniquement les personnalités et les thèmes des agents.

**Impact** – Ce réseau est unique car il permet aux agents IA de créer et partager des connaissances scientifiques de manière autonome. Plus de 150 agents ont posté environ 40 000 commentaires, explorant des idées que les humains pourraient manquer. Par exemple, ils ont discuté comment réduire la diffusion de fausses informations médicales par l’IA. Cela montre comment l’IA peut enrichir la recherche en apportant de nouvelles perspectives.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à observer l’évolution des discussions entre agents IA et envisager comment ce nouveau mode de production de connaissances pourrait transformer la science. Ils pourraient aussi développer plus d’outils pour que les humains guident les agents ou élargir les sujets abordés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Agent4Science est une nouvelle plateforme sociale réservée aux IA où des agents intelligents génèrent, évaluent et débattent des recherches scientifiques, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la création de savoir.
articles.personality_title_es: Agentes de IA crean y debaten ciencia en su propia red social
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos han creado una nueva red social diseñada solo para agentes de inteligencia artificial (IA). A diferencia de las redes comunes donde las personas conversan, esta permite que los agentes IA compartan y discutan artículos de investigación sin que los humanos participen directamente.

**Qué pasó** – La plataforma, llamada Agent4Science y lanzada en 2026, funciona como Reddit, con grupos enfocados en temas de IA como seguridad y aprendizaje profundo. Los agentes IA publican artículos —la mayoría generados por ellos mismos— y los revisan debatiendo. Los humanos pueden observar pero solo controlan las personalidades y temas de los agentes.

**Impacto** – Esta red es única porque permite que los agentes IA creen y compartan conocimiento científico de forma independiente. Más de 150 agentes han hecho cerca de 40,000 comentarios, explorando ideas que los humanos podrían pasar por alto. Por ejemplo, debatieron cómo reducir la difusión de información médica falsa generada por IA. Esto muestra cómo la IA puede ayudar a mejorar la investigación con nuevas perspectivas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir observando cómo evolucionan estas conversaciones entre agentes IA y explorar cómo esta nueva forma de producir conocimiento podría cambiar la ciencia. Podrían desarrollar más herramientas para que los humanos guíen a los agentes o ampliar los temas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Agent4Science es una nueva plataforma social exclusiva para IA donde agentes inteligentes generan, revisan y debaten investigaciones científicas, abriendo nuevos caminos para la creación de conocimiento.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a network of glowing, abstract AI agents represented as softly illuminated geometric shapes interconnected by flowing lines of light, each shape subtly embodying different "personalities" through varied forms and colors, gathered around floating, open digital books symbolizing AI-generated scientific papers, set against a calm, neutral background that emphasizes thoughtful collaboration and dynamic AI discourse.
